claim: LLMs have a 'smart zone' and 'dumb zone': token accumulation causes quadratic scaling of attention relationships, degrading quality around 100K tokens. Stay within the smart zone by sizing tasks to fit within ~100K context.
actionable: When working with an LLM on a task, monitor token count closely. Split work into pieces that stay under 100K tokens to maintain quality. Clear context and restart rather than continuously expanding the same session.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Use a 'Grill Me' skill to reach shared understanding with AI before planning: ask relentless questions about requirements to align on a design concept rather than jumping to plan generation.
actionable: Before implementing, invoke a grill-style skill with your brief. Answer the AI's clarifying questions methodically (can take 20-80+ questions). This conversation history becomes your design concept asset.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Reject 'specs to code' movement: feeding a specification document to AI without understanding the resulting code creates uncontrollable iteration loops. Keep your hands on the codebase.
actionable: Do not pass a spec to AI and then only edit the spec when output is wrong. Instead, review and shape the actual code. Use specs as alignment tools, not compilation inputs.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: LLMs are 'like Memento'—they forget context completely when cleared. Prefer clearing context and restarting fresh (staying in smart zone) over 'compacting' conversation into a summary.
actionable: Design workflows to clear context and restart fresh from the system prompt rather than building long conversation histories. This keeps the LLM predictable and in the smart zone.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Keep system prompts as tiny as possible (not 250K tokens). Every token in the system prompt consumes smart-zone capacity before the task even starts.
actionable: Audit your system prompt. Remove redundant instructions, boilerplate, and historical context. Aim for minimal size so the LLM enters the smart zone ready to work on your actual task.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Break work into vertical slices (tracer bullets) that cross all system layers, not horizontal slices by layer. AI naturally codes horizontally and misses integration feedback until late.
actionable: When slicing issues, ensure each slice touches database schema, API, and UI. Example: 'Award points for lesson completion visible on dashboard' (not 'Create gamification service, then API layer, then UI'). This gives AI feedback on the full vertical flow early.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Use a Kanban board with explicit blocking relationships instead of sequential multi-phase plans to enable parallel agent work and visibility of dependencies.
actionable: When breaking down a PRD into issues, create a Kanban board showing which tasks block which. Use this to parallelize: independent tasks can be grabbed by multiple agents simultaneously.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Test-Driven Development (TDD) with red-green-refactor is essential for AI coding. Writing the test first prevents the AI from cheating by ignoring the test layer.
actionable: Instruct the AI to write a failing test before implementation. This forces it to satisfy the test contract, not just write code. AI tends to cheat when test-writing happens after implementation.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Code quality and test coverage directly ceiling the AI's output quality. If feedback loops are weak (bad tests, no type checking), AI will produce bad code regardless of prompts.
actionable: Before delegating more work to AI, audit your test suite and type coverage. Strengthen feedback loops (tests, linting, type checks). This is the primary lever for improving AI code quality.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Design modules as 'deep' (small interface, large functionality) not 'shallow' (large interface, small functionality). Deep modules are easier for AI to test and navigate.
actionable: When designing modules for AI work, create large cohesive services with simple public interfaces (e.g., GamificationService) rather than many tiny, highly-interdependent files. This makes tests and AI logic clearer.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Retain architectural awareness by designing module interfaces but delegating implementation ('gray box' approach). This keeps you in control of shape without reviewing every line.
actionable: Define interfaces and contracts for new modules upfront. Delegate implementation to AI. Review only the interface behavior and contract compliance, not internal logic, to reduce review burden while maintaining architectural control.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: PRDs are alignment documents, not review artifacts. Don't spend time re-reading and perfecting PRDs; the value is in the grilling conversation. Mark PRD issues as closed to signal completion and prevent doc rot.
actionable: After writing a PRD with AI, don't review it. The grilling session already validated alignment. Mark the PRD issue as closed in GitHub so AI doesn't re-discover and reuse stale documentation.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Doc rot occurs when old PRDs and specifications remain in repos and influence AI on later changes. Delete or mark as closed after implementation to prevent outdated guidance.
actionable: After closing an issue/PRD, delete the markdown file or mark the GitHub issue as closed. This prevents AI from discovering and re-using documentation that's now out of sync with the actual codebase.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: For code review standards, use 'push' (instructions in system prompt/Claude.md) for the reviewer and 'pull' (skills available in repo) for the implementer. Reviewer needs explicit standards, implementer discovers them.
actionable: Create a skill for coding standards. Push this skill (via explicit instructions) to the reviewer agent so it knows the standards to check. Let the implementer pull it if needed. This asymmetry improves code quality.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Parallelization with agents requires directed acyclic graphs of issues with blocking relationships, not sequential numbered phases. Use a planner agent to identify independent work for parallel execution.
actionable: Use Sandcastle or similar tools to run a planner agent that identifies parallelizable issues, then spawn multiple implementer agents on those issues simultaneously. Merge results afterward.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: QA is where human taste and oversight matter most; don't try to fully automate planning, research, or QA. Automated systems without human review tend to produce 'slop' lacking intentionality.
actionable: Reserve QA and final architectural decisions for humans. Use AI for implementation and code generation. Review all AI outputs before merge. This guards code quality and design coherence.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Hosting context in isolated sub-agents (delegated tasks) keeps main orchestrator context lean. Sub-agent reports summary back, so you get insights without token bloat in your main session.
actionable: When a task requires heavy exploration (e.g., 90K tokens), delegate to a sub-agent instead of doing it in your main session. Let the sub-agent summarize findings. Main agent stays in smart zone.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Use 'improve code base architecture' skill to identify modules that should be deeper and more testable. Treat architecture improvement as a continuous activity, not a one-time design phase.
actionable: Periodically run an architecture review skill on your codebase to identify shallow modules and coupling issues. Create issues to refactor toward deep modules and better test boundaries.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Clear the context (not compact) before review phases. A reviewer in a fresh, smart-zone context will be smarter than one reviewing in a dumb zone where implementation history accumulated.
actionable: For code review, clear context and restart with a fresh prompt + code diff, not the full conversation history. This keeps the reviewer in the smart zone where it can spot subtler issues.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock

claim: Software engineering fundamentals from pre-AI books (Pragmatic Programmer, Design of Design, etc.) apply directly to AI workflows. They teach principles like tracer bullets, deep modules, and vertical slices.
actionable: Re-read classic software engineering books (Pragmatic Programmer, Design of Design by John Ousterhout). Translate principles to AI workflows: tracer bullets, vertical slices, deep modules, small tasks.
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Workflow for AI Coding — Matt Pocock
